concept: article plus host contribution
prompt: In ٱلْحَمْدُ, what visible piece marks definiteness, and why is duplicating 'the' unsafe?
expected_answer: The prefixed ال marks definiteness and the host is حمد; the hover should account for the article once, not duplicate it as 'the + the praise'.
accepted_variants: ال marks the; host is hamd/praise; do not duplicate the
forbidden_answers: the + the praise
the article is decoration
root vibes are enough

concept: content-letter harakah
prompt: Which is 'did not' and which is 'why': لَمْ / لِمَ? Name the deciding feature.
expected_answer: لَمْ means did not; لِمَ means why. The kasra/fatha on the lām distinguishes the particles.
accepted_variants: lam means did not and lima means why; the vowel on lam decides
forbidden_answers: they are interchangeable
norm key is enough

concept: definite common noun
prompt: In ٱلنَّاسُ, what is the article and what is the host noun?
expected_answer: ال is the definite article and ناس is the common noun host meaning people/humankind in context.
accepted_variants: article ال plus noun ناس
the people/humankind, with ال as article
forbidden_answers: the whole word is only a particle
ناس is a verb here
the article is an extra the

concept: finite passive verb, not dictionary infinitive
prompt: Why is an infinitive hover like 'to create' unsafe for خُلِقَ?
expected_answer: خُلِقَ is a finite passive perfect verb, so the hover must preserve the finite/passive contribution, such as 'was created', not an entry infinitive.
accepted_variants: finite passive perfect; was created, not to create
forbidden_answers: to create
root خ ل ق is enough
voice does not matter

concept: preposition-host contribution
prompt: Why is a host-only hover for بِسَلَامٍ unsafe?
expected_answer: The prefixed bāʾ contributes the prepositional relation, so the hover cannot show only the host noun peace.
accepted_variants: it must account for bāʾ plus salām
with/in/by peace by context, not host-only peace
forbidden_answers: peace only
the bāʾ is decoration
required_reasoning: relation not copied from external wording

concept: وما decomposition and function
prompt: Why is وَمَا one written token but not one flat grammar piece?
expected_answer: It is one Qurʾānic word token, but it contains wāw plus mā; mā must be classified by context, such as negative, relative, interrogative, or maṣdariyyah.
accepted_variants: one written token, multiple grammar pieces; function decides mā
forbidden_answers: always means and what
always means and not
required_reasoning: mā function left pending if context is missing

concept: finite verb plus object suffix with adjacent subject context
prompt: A hover for يَسْأَلُكَ says only 'to ask, question' or only 'ask you'. What must a rich answer distinguish in 33:63?
expected_answer: The token contributes ask you: a finite imperfect verb plus attached second-person masculine singular object pronoun. The contextual phrase may be the people ask you because النَّاسُ supplies the explicit subject in the following word, not because they is inside يَسْأَلُكَ.
accepted_variants: token contribution ask you; contextual phrase the people ask you from following النَّاسُ
finite verb + ka object suffix; subject is supplied by quran:33:63:2
forbidden_answers: to ask, question
ask with hidden suffix only
they is an attached pronoun inside this token
people is inside يَسْأَلُكَ
required_reasoning: initial imperfect prefix is morphology, not a lexical he
token contribution and contextual phrase gloss separated
following النَّاسُ named as subject source

concept: component-only evidence is not whole-token certification
prompt: A rich segmenter detects وَ + ال + host in وَالشَّجَرُ. What can and cannot follow from that?
expected_answer: The component evidence can support display and review, but it cannot certify the whole-token hover or parse-family propagation without the sarf/nahw gate.
accepted_variants: component candidates help routing, not whole-token certification
forbidden_answers: all وَالـ nouns are auto safe
the segmenter alone certifies the hover
required_reasoning: component evidence separated from whole token

concept: exception frame
prompt: Why can إِلَّا require hard-grammar review before a hover is rich-certified?
expected_answer: The exception particle must be read with the main noun and excepted noun frame; positive/negative and connected/disconnected exception affect case and reasoning.
accepted_variants: إلا needs the exception frame and case reasoning
forbidden_answers: it always means except and nothing else matters

concept: visible dual ending
prompt: A hover for بُرْهَٰنَانِ says 'two proofs' but shows one plain noun span. What is missing?
expected_answer: The host noun and the visible dual ending must both be represented. The learner should see that the dual ending, not only the English word two, carries the number.
accepted_variants: host plus dual suffix; show the dual ending
the dual ending must be visible in the color/breakdown
forbidden_answers: one plain noun span is enough
dual is only an English word
root family is enough
required_reasoning: display class or morphline teaches number

concept: finite imperfect prefix visibility
prompt: In يُحْيِي, why is one undifferentiated verb color not enough for a rich hover?
expected_answer: The initial يُ is an imperfect verb prefix and the remaining host is the lexical verb stem. The hover should show prefix plus stem and avoid confusing this finite verb with a proper-name family.
accepted_variants: split ي prefix from verb stem
imperfect prefix must be visible
forbidden_answers: one verb span is enough
this is only a name
the prefix is decoration

concept: passive participle / adjective not infinitive
prompt: A hover for مُّطَاعٍۢ says 'to obey' and leaves the Arabic host unmarked. What should the learner answer?
expected_answer: This is a passive participle or adjectival token in context, so the contribution is a quality such as obedient or obeyed by context, not the infinitive to obey. The derivative shape should be visible.
accepted_variants: passive participle/adjective, not infinitive
show derivative prefix and adjectival contribution
forbidden_answers: to obey
white host is enough
root alone decides

concept: passive verb with subject/object suffixes
prompt: For أُورِثْتُمُوهَا, what must a rich hover expose before the gloss can be trusted?
expected_answer: It must expose the passive perfect host, the second-person plural marker, and the attached object pronoun ها. A fluent phrase is unsafe if it hides passive voice or the object pronoun.
accepted_variants: passive perfect plus 2mp marker plus object ha
show passive host, subject/deputy-subject marker, and object pronoun
forbidden_answers: inherited only
the object is not in the token
a host-only verb color is enough

concept: deterministic Qur'an display before rich hover
prompt: A usage card loses a hamza seat, maddah, diacritic, or selected word boundary before segmentation. What is the correct state?
expected_answer: Stop before grammar certification. The displayed Qur'an text must be repaired from the canonical project source before root, pattern, particle function, or color segmentation is trusted.
accepted_variants: source display mismatch blocks rich hover
repair canonical display before segmentation
forbidden_answers: segment the damaged display anyway
guess from memory
hover can fix the text later

concept: learner explanation must teach Arabic, not workflow
prompt: A tooltip explanation says the hover is authored from Qamus data and keeps internal evidence out of the public payload. Why is that wrong for the learner?
expected_answer: That sentence is process prose, not Arabic instruction. The learner explanation should name what each Arabic piece contributes and move process or gate statements to reports or admin views.
accepted_variants: learner prose should teach Arabic pieces, not deployment/evidence process
move process text out of hover
forbidden_answers: process prose is a good learner explanation
public-boundary text belongs in the tooltip
required_reasoning: public/admin gate text separated from learner hover

concept: graph edge join before rollout claims
prompt: A tranche report says all rows are complete, but a visible example card still has flat words. What must the tutor or agent ask for before accepting the claim?
expected_answer: Ask for the edge join table and four denominators: entries, listed example cards, visible selected words, and rich-live words. Each visible card must be fully live or explicitly blocked with the missing edge or next action.
accepted_variants: entry/card/visible-word/rich-live denominators are required
build the graph edge join first and name missing edges
forbidden_answers: row count alone proves completion
manual page checking is enough when graph edges exist
blocked visible cards can be omitted from the denominator
required_reasoning: entry to card to word edge chain named

concept: cachebuster and source/runtime parity before public color claims
prompt: A color-class fix was committed, but public pages may still serve old assets or an old payload. What proof is needed before saying the learner-visible hover is fixed?
expected_answer: Record the asset version or cachebuster in public HTML, fetch the affected CSS or JS with that version, verify source/runtime payload parity, then read back the public DOM or hover. Without those checks, the fix may not be visible to learners.
accepted_variants: need cachebuster/asset readback plus source-runtime parity and public DOM check
commit alone does not prove public color changed
forbidden_answers: git commit is enough
restart alone proves it
old cached CSS does not matter
required_reasoning: public DOM or hover readback named

concept: nominal vs verbal sentence type and subject
prompt: You are shown five short sentences. How do you label each as nominal or verbal, and where do you look first for the subject in each?
expected_answer: A sentence that opens with a noun is nominal: its subject is that opening noun (the mubtada). A sentence that opens with a verb is verbal: its subject is the doer that follows the verb. Read the opener first, then locate the subject by that type.
accepted_variants: noun-opener = nominal (subject is the opening noun); verb-opener = verbal (subject is the doer after the verb)
label by the first word's type, then find the mubtada in nominal or the doer in verbal
forbidden_answers: every sentence is verbal
the subject is always the first word regardless of type
type does not change where the subject is
required_reasoning: nominal vs verbal label assigned from the opener
subject located by sentence type, not by position alone

concept: doer and object in a verbal sentence
prompt: In a verbal sentence you have a verb, then two nouns. How do you decide which noun is the doer and which is the object, and what tells you?
expected_answer: The doer is read in the nominative and the object in the accusative; the visible ending on each noun decides the role, not the word order alone. The doer follows the verb and the object follows the doer, but you confirm by reading the ending.
accepted_variants: doer is nominative, object is accusative; the ending decides the role
confirm doer vs object by the visible ending, not only by position
forbidden_answers: the first noun is always the doer no matter the ending
the ending does not matter once you know the meaning
swap them if the English sounds better

concept: narration-frame register vs reported-content register
prompt: A narration has a chain frame (the report-of-transmission vocabulary) followed by the reported content. Why must you read the frame as its own register before reading the content, and what goes wrong if you flatten both into ordinary prose?
expected_answer: The chain frame uses a fixed transmission register such as the recurring narrated-to-us verb and the linking prepositions between names; that register is read as frame, not as part of the reported sentence. If you flatten the frame into ordinary prose you misread the transmission verbs as content and lose the boundary between who-reported and what-was-reported.
accepted_variants: read the transmission frame as its own register, then the reported content separately
the chain-of-transmission vocabulary is a frame register, not part of the reported sentence
forbidden_answers: the frame and the content are one flat sentence
the transmission verbs are ordinary content verbs
register does not affect how you read it
required_reasoning: frame separated from the reported content
no actual narration text invented or quoted

concept: transmission-frame verbs are register markers, not content claims
prompt: When a recurring narrated-to-us / reported-from verb appears in the chain frame, why is it wrong to gloss it as if it were a content verb of the reported sentence, and how should a careful reader treat it?
expected_answer: Those verbs belong to the transmission register; they mark who passed the report along, not an action inside the reported content. A careful reader keeps them in the frame register, names them as transmission markers, and does not let them bleed into the gloss of the reported sentence.
accepted_variants: transmission verbs are frame register markers, not content of the report
keep the narrated-to-us verb in the frame; do not gloss it as reported-sentence content
forbidden_answers: the narrated-to-us verb is part of the reported sentence
frame verbs and content verbs read the same way
the chain frame can be ignored entirely
required_reasoning: verb placed in the transmission frame register
frame verb kept out of the reported-content gloss

concept: name a precise PENDING reason instead of a vague blank
prompt: Reading an unseen paragraph, you reach a block you cannot clear. Why is a vague 'I don't know' not an acceptable answer, and what must you produce instead?
expected_answer: A vague blank teaches nothing and cannot be routed. You must name the precise pending reason from the shared vocabulary, such as homograph_haraka, pos_mismatch, multi_sense, contronym, referent_unresolved, proper_name, or seat_collapsed, and then point to the procedure that clears that exact reason. A precise blank beats a confident wrong.
accepted_variants: name the exact pending reason code and the procedure that clears it, not a vague blank
self-diagnose with a specific reason such as referent_unresolved or pos_mismatch, then route it
forbidden_answers: just write I don't know and move on
guess a gloss to fill the blank
a vague blank is the same as a named pending
required_reasoning: precise pending reason named from the shared vocabulary

concept: recognize a proper name and stop inflecting it
prompt: In an unseen paragraph a word resists every root and pattern you try. Why might the right pending reason be proper_name, and what does naming it correctly stop you from doing?
expected_answer: When a word will not yield a root or pattern, it may be a proper name rather than a derivable form; the correct pending reason is proper_name. Naming it stops you from forcing a fake root onto a name and inflecting it as if it were an ordinary noun or verb, and it routes you to confirm the name instead of guessing meaning.
accepted_variants: name proper_name and stop forcing a root onto a name
if no root/pattern fits, suspect a proper name; do not inflect it
forbidden_answers: every word must have a root to inflect
force the closest root and gloss it anyway
a name and an ordinary noun are read the same way
